text: Phenomenologist Dan Zahavi distinguishes between 'minimal self' and 'narrative self.' The narrative self — the one that tells your life story, worries about reputation, plans next month — is almost never present. It lives in abstracted time. The minimal self, by contrast, is the bare first-person character of any lived experience: the felt quality of 'this is happening to me, now.' Zahavi argues that distraction does not erase the minimal self; it merely layers the narrative self on top, creating a persistent hum of mental commentary that prevents experience from being registered with its full phenomenal weight. Silence that commentary and the minimal self resurfaces.
question: According to Dan Zahavi, what does distraction do to the minimal self?

options.text: It layers the narrative self on top, muffling the phenomenal weight of experience
It erases the minimal self entirely, leaving only cognitive processing
It strengthens the minimal self by forcing it to compete for attention
It transfers the minimal self into long-term memory storage
